nativeRange.habitat: Rocky and reef-like areas rich in algae along the tropical west-African coast. Commonly observed between roughly 20–40 m, with the broader recorded depth range of about 3–70 m.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on benthic hard-shelled invertebrates — molluscs, small crustaceans, worms — using an elongated snout to root through sediment and strong jaws to crush prey. In captivity has been observed to accept a varied carnivore diet including vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, krill, finely chopped clam or shrimp, and quality marine pellets and flakes.

summary: A large East-Atlantic wrasse from algae-rich rocky reefs of west Africa, reaching about 45–50 cm. Generally considered reef-safe with caution and best suited to very large fish-only or fish-tolerant systems.
careNotes:
The Blackbar Hogfish (Bodianus speciosus) is a large wrasse endemic to the tropical eastern central Atlantic, ranging from Cameroon north to Guinea and offshore to the Cape Verde Islands. Juveniles are pale with a black spot on the dorsal fin and tend to be relatively easy in captivity; as fish mature they darken and develop the species' distinctive vertical black bar behind the pectoral fin, and adults become considerably more assertive.

Reported maximum size varies meaningfully across sources: FishBase records up to 50 cm SL (common length about 40 cm TL), LiveAquaria states 16 inches (~40 cm), BlueZoo states 19 inches (~48 cm), and Reeflex cites 30–50 cm. The 35–50 cm range used here reflects the averaged adult-size spread, with the upper bound representing the well-documented FishBase maximum.

Tank-size recommendations spread widely. BlueZoo lists 100 US gallons (~378 L), LiveAquaria 300 US gallons (~1,136 L), and Reeflex states the species 'is not suitable for home aquaria' at all. For an active 40–50 cm wrasse that grows quickly and needs ample swimming room, a 1,000 L (≈265 US gal) system with a long footprint of roughly 240 cm is treated here as a sensible long-term minimum; smaller systems are likely to be marginal for full adults, and going larger is generally preferable. This sizing applies caution-bias per the playbook because of the >2× source spread.

Reef compatibility is best described as 'with caution'. Adults have strong crushing jaws and have been documented preying on small crustaceans, worms, snails and small bivalves; corals are not typically targeted. If kept with a reef, introduce the hogfish last and avoid stocking small motile invertebrates afterward.

Like most wrasses, this species is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite, and as with other large hogfishes a tightly-sealed lid is generally advised — hogfishes are commonly reported jumpers.

Cross-source agreement was good on temperament (semi-aggressive), diet (carnivore), reef-with-caution rating, and water parameters (LiveAquaria: 72–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12; FishBase preferred range 20.2–27.9 °C; values written here are averaged across these). Size and tank size showed the largest source spread; both were resolved on the cautious-larger side as the playbook prescribes.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous with distinct pair formation during spawning. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, individuals typically begin life as females, with dominant individuals transitioning to male — single-sex captive groups have been observed to undergo sex change. Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species detail page lists: 16 in adult, 300 gal minimum, 72–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, semi-aggressive, reef-safe with caution, carnivore, easy care level, rocky algae-rich reef habitat.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked max body length (50 cm SL, common ~40 cm TL), taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria/misc, subfamily Hypsigenyinae), Eastern Central Atlantic distribution (Cameroon to Guinea + Cape Verde), depth range (3–70 m, typical 20–40 m), preferred temperature (20.2–27.9 °C, mean 25.9 °C), oviparous distinct-pair spawning, protogynous hermaphrodite, IUCN Data Deficient (2008).

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (19 in / ~48 cm), tank minimum (100 gal — lowest of all sources, treated as under-stated for a 40+ cm active wrasse), semi-aggressive temperament, reef-with-caution, carnivore diet, Eastern Atlantic origin, alternate trade name 'Purple and Yellow Hogfish'.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (30–50 cm), temperature (21–28 °C), carnivorous diet (clam, mysis, krill, shrimp, frozen meaty foods), West-African distribution, protogynous hermaphrodite. Reeflex flags the species as 'not suitable for home aquaria' due to size — used to bias the tank-size choice toward the cautious-larger end of the cross-source spread.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Confirmed Bodianus speciosus identity and accepts both live and frozen food; care indicators range 'easy to moderate' and 'social to aggressive', consistent with the intermediate / semi-aggressive baseline written here. Page had limited numeric husbandry detail.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Attempted as the first secondary source per source-urls.json (fish-saltwater slice); returned HTTP 403 and could not be used. Cross-verification was instead completed via FishBase, BlueZoo Aquatics, Reeflex, and Quality Marine.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums. Wild spawning is oviparous with distinct pair formation; the species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite.
